text: The robotic-assisted procedure begins with the posterior mobilization of the rectum, revealing the cyst's proximity to the mesorectum. The dissection plane avoids sacral involvement, facilitating identification of anatomical landmarks.

The surgeon identifies the rectum on the right side, the left posterior aspect of the cyst, and the left lateral aspect where the pelvic plexus is located. The left posterior aspect of the levators, including the coccygeus and iliococcygeus muscles, is also identified. The fascia of the lateral compartment with the embedded pelvic plexus is visible, with the cyst not invading it.

The surgeon examines the lateral part of the mesorectum, identifying the tethered surface due to the cyst abutting the pelvic plexus. Subtle fibrosis is visible between the cyst within the mesorectum and the dome of the iliococcygeal muscle. The cyst exerts pressure on the left lateral compartment, the left aspect of the vagina, and the left neurovascular bundle. A well-defined capsule of the cyst is observed, guiding the dissection line.

The surgeon uses the vaginal surface as a reference point to perform resection medially and laterally. A clear dissection plane is established between the cyst and the left side of the pelvis, although some adherence is observed near the left levators. The thin wall of the cyst poses a risk of rupture, but partial decompression helps delineate the obscure aspects of the cyst wall.

The surgeon uses flexible sigmoidoscopy to distend and collapse the rectum, visualizing the dissection plane between the cyst wall and the rectum. Simultaneous traction and counter traction are applied between the cyst wall and the rectum using the robotic platform, facilitating the dissection. The cyst is strongly adhered to the medial side of the levators and the rectal wall, particularly near the anal rectal junction. The entire cyst is successfully removed without leaving fragments of the cyst wall.

The surgeon removes the cyst using an endoscopic retrieval bag through the robotic port. A Jackson-Pratt drain is placed in the pelvis to manage postoperative fluid. The surgical field is clear, and the procedure concludes with the placement of the drain.
